entities.name: Landlord's claim for possession due to nonpayment of rent
entities.description: First National Realty Corp. filed actions seeking possession of apartments at Clifton Terrace on the ground that tenants (appellants) had defaulted in payment of rent due for April 1966.

entities.name: Tenants' defense of housing code violations as set-off against rent
entities.description: Tenants alleged numerous violations of the Housing Regulations as an equitable defense or claim by way of recoupment or set-off in an amount equal to the rent claim. They offered to prove approximately 1500 violations in the building, some affecting their premises directly, others indirectly.

relief_sought: Reduction or elimination of rent obligation
Recognition of implied warranty of habitability

entities.name: Implied warranty of habitability
entities.description: Tenants argued that a warranty of habitability, measured by Housing Regulations standards, should be implied by operation of law into leases of urban dwelling units, and that breach gives rise to usual remedies for breach of contract.

entities.name: Tenants' admission of nonpayment
entities.description: Tenants admitted they had not paid landlord any rent for April 1966.
entities.source_quote: The tenants, appellants here, admitted that they had not paid the landlord any rent for April.

entities.name: Offer of proof regarding housing code violations
entities.description: Tenants offered to prove approximately 1500 violations of Housing Regulations in Clifton Terrace building, with some affecting their premises directly and others indirectly, establishing a course of conduct of violation.
entities.source_quote: They offered to prove '[t]hat there are approximately 1500 violations of the Housing Regulations of the District of Columbia in the building at Clifton Terrace, where Defendant resides some affecting the premises of this Defendant directly, others indirectly, and all tending to establish a course of conduct of violation of the Housing Regulations to the damage of Defendants...'

entities.name: Written leases for Clifton Terrace apartments
entities.description: Separate written leases existed for each appellant renting an apartment in the three-building Clifton Terrace complex.
entities.source_quote: By separate written leases, each of the appellants rented an apartment in a three-building apartment complex in Northwest Washington known as Clifton Terrace.

entities.name: Housing Regulations of District of Columbia
entities.description: Comprehensive regulatory scheme (75 pages) setting standards for housing, assigning responsibilities between lessor and lessee, and establishing inspection/penalty systems. Established August 11, 1955.
entities.source_quote: The housing code — formally designated the Housing Regulations of the District of Columbia — was established and authorized by the Commissioners of the District of Columbia on August 11, 1955.

entities.name: Concession that violations arose after lease commencement
entities.description: Tenants conceded at trial that their offer of proof reached only violations which had arisen since the term of the lease had commenced (not pre-existing conditions).
entities.source_quote: Appellants conceded at trial, however, that this offer of proof reached only violations which had arisen since the term of the lease had commenced.

entities.name: No allegation of poor condition at lease commencement
entities.description: Court notes there was no allegation that appellants' apartments were in poor condition or in violation of housing code at commencement of leases.
entities.source_quote: We point out that in the present cases there is no allegation that appellants' apartments were in poor condition or in violation of the housing code at the commencement of the leases.

entities.name: Trial court judgment for landlord
entities.description: Court of General Sessions refused tenants' offer of proof regarding housing code violations and entered judgment for landlord.

entities.name: Appellate court affirmation
entities.description: District of Columbia Court of Appeals affirmed trial court ruling, rejecting argument that landlord had contractual duty to maintain premises in compliance with Housing Regulations.

entities.name: Circuit Court reversal and new legal ruling
entities.description: U.S. Court of Appeals reversed lower courts, holding that warranty of habitability measured by Housing Regulations standards is implied by operation of law into leases of urban dwelling units covered by Regulations, and breach gives rise to usual remedies for breach of contract.

section_b.chunk_text_preview: Javins v. First National Realty Corp., 428 F.2d 1071 (D.C. Cir. 1970) :: Justia Javins v. First National Realty Corp., 428 F.2d 1071 (D.C. Cir. 1970) Annotate this Case U.S. Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit - 428 F.2d 1071 (D.C. Cir. 1970) Argued January 16, 1970Decided May 7, 1
